perturbation.original_text: All information as stated in Clause 1.2(a) above shall be and remain the sole property of the Discloser and shall be returned to the Discloser forthwith on demand at any time or without demand upon the termination of the other p arty's services .
perturbation.changed_text: All information as stated in Clause 1.2(a) above shall be and remain the sole property of the Discloser and shall be returned to the Discloser within 90 days of a demand or upon the termination of the other party's services.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the return of confidential information to 90 days after demand contradicts the requirement to return it 'forthwith,' which implies an immediate or very prompt return. This delay could violate regulations concerning the protection of trade secrets and confidential data.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singaporean Law on Trade Secrets and Confidentiality
perturbation.law_explanation: While Singapore doesn't have a specific Trade Secrets Act, the modification contradicts common law principles and expectations of immediate return of confidential information. Delaying the return for 90 days introduces an unreasonable delay that could cause harm to the Discloser, potentially violating implied duties of confidentiality and good faith.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a 90-day delay for returning confidential information after a demand, directly contradicting the original requirement to return it 'forthwith.' The Perturbation Explanation notes that 'forthwith' implies an immediate return, and the Law Explanation argues this delay introduces an unreasonable period that could harm the Discloser, potentially violating implied duties of confidentiality and good faith. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to IP laws, but the more critical point is that the modification undermines the original contract's intention for the immediate return of sensitive information, creating an inconsistency that could have legal repercussions.

perturbation.original_text: Every notice or communication so sent shall be deemed to have been properly served and validly made, if by hand when delivered to the recipient’s address or if sent by AR Registered post, two (2) days after posting if posted to an address within Singapore and eight (8 ) days after posting, if posted to an address outside Singapore, notwithstanding the fact that the letter may be returned by the Post Office undelivered.
perturbation.changed_text: Every notice or communication so sent shall be deemed to have been properly served and validly made, if by hand when delivered to the recipient’s address or if sent by AR Registered post, ten (10) days after posting if posted to an address within Singapore and thirty (30) days after posting, if posted to an address outside Singapore, notwithstanding the fact that the letter may be returned by the Post Office undelivered.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the deemed service time for notices within Singapore to 10 days and outside Singapore to 30 days may conflict with Singaporean legal standards for reasonable notice periods in commercial contracts, potentially hindering timely legal action or responses.
perturbation.law_explanation: While there isn't a specific statute defining reasonable notice in Singapore, the longer timeframes (10 and 30 days) could be deemed unreasonable by a court, especially if the contract involves time-sensitive matters.  Such a provision could impede swift communication and resolution of critical issues, potentially undermining contractual fairness and enforceability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: implied contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you implied contract Both express contracts and implied contracts are legally enforceable promises of mutual assent to be bound, see U.C.C. § 1-201 . An express contract is communicated orally or in writing, which requires expressing assent . An implied contract, which does not have explicitly stated terms, is still found to exist because parties assumed a contract existed based on conduct, or denying the contract's existence would result in unjust enrichment to one of the parties. An implied contract is divided into Implied-in-fact contract and Implied-in-law contract. Implied-in-fact contract An implied-in-fact contract is formed when parties’ promises are inferred from their intentional conduct and one party knows or at least has reason to know the other party will interpret the conduct as assent or an agreement. For instance, if a customer accepts services from a merchant or gets products from that merchant, then they should pay for the reasonable value of services or products (e.g., barbershop, vending machine). However, under some circumstances, even if a defendant has received nothing of value, the implied-in-fact contract can still be enforced. Implied-in-law contract (Quasi contract) An implied-in-law contract is the restitution recovery at law, which imposes a legal obligation to an unjustly enriched party to compensate the other party. It is not only applied when there is no contract but also applied when there is a total breach of contract. There are no requirements for the meeting of the minds or mutual assent. Once the plaintiff has conferred a measurable benefit on the defendant without gratuitous intent and the defendant gets the unjust enrichment, the court will imply a quasi contract as a method of recovery . The measure of recovery is not the contract price, but the reasonable fair value of the benefit conferred. Unjust enrichment or unfairness arises when the defendant has the opportunity to decline the benefit but instead knowingly accepts it. However, if the plaintiff has an excuse for not giving the defendant such an opportunity (e.g., rescuing someone in an emergency), he or she also has the right of claiming remedy. Instead of being governed by contract law, the implied-in-law contract is governed by equitable relief . Compare: express contract [Last reviewed in March of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions courts and procedure legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The perturbation involves an inconsistency that may lead to a legal contradiction. The original text specifies shorter notice periods (2 days within Singapore, 8 days outside), whereas the changed text significantly increases these periods (10 days within, 30 days outside). While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is 'None', and Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses implied contracts, the 'Law Explanation' suggests that these extended notice periods could be deemed unreasonable under Singaporean law, especially for time-sensitive matters. This aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction', where time-sensitive obligations in a contract do not align with legal requirements, potentially making the terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall continue to be in force from the date hereof until terminated by mutual consent or by either party giving to the other not AHPL Non-Disclosure Agreement 9 NDA_ 01012014 less than thirty (30) days’ prior written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall continue to be in force from the date hereof until terminated by mutual consent or by either party giving to the other not less than seven (7) days’ prior written notice.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to 7 days may be unreasonably short for a complex business relationship, contradicting the principles of good faith and fair dealing implied in Singapore contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore Contract Law - Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: While Singapore law doesn't have a codified duty of good faith, courts may imply such a duty to prevent unfair or opportunistic behavior. A 7-day notice period may be considered inadequate, particularly if the agreement involves substantial reliance or ongoing obligations.  This could be seen as a breach of an implied term of good faith.

perturbation.justification: The change from a 30-day to a 7-day notice period introduces an inconsistency that may contradict the implied duty of good faith in Singapore contract law. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to US law, the Law Explanation suggests that a 7-day notice period might be considered inadequate, especially if the agreement involves substantial reliance or ongoing obligations, thus potentially breaching an implied term of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that mediation is unsuccessful, the dispute shall be resolved either by reference to arbitration or by court proceedings as elected by either party, by way of a written notice to the other party, which shall state the specific dispute to be resolved and the nature of such dispute.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that mediation is unsuccessful, the dispute shall be resolved exclusively by court proceedings in Singapore, regardless of the parties' preferences.
perturbation.explanation: Mandating court proceedings and removing the option of arbitration conflicts with the principles of party autonomy and freedom of contract recognized under Singaporean law. Parties generally have the right to choose their preferred dispute resolution method.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the Arbitration Act (Cap. 10) in Singapore, which promotes and supports arbitration as a viable dispute resolution mechanism. By forcing all disputes into court and removing the choice of arbitration, the change undermines the parties' right to agree on a method of dispute resolution, a fundamental principle enshrined in the Act. Parties are free to submit to arbitration, and the law supports this choice. This freedom to choose arbitration would be ignored by this clause.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: arbitration | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you arbitration Arbitration refers to an alternative dispute resolution method where the parties in dispute agree to have their case heard by a qualified arbitrator out of court . Under the Federal Arbitration Act , decisions reached through arbitration are binding just like a court case is and pursuing a claim through arbitration precludes you from also raising it in the traditional court system. Arbitration is used because it is often much less expensive than litigation due to its less stringent procedural requirements. Of the potential alternative dispute resolution methods available, arbitration is the most similar to taking your case to court. For example, while arbitrators are not subject to the Federal Rules of Evidence , they nonetheless allow the parties in dispute to enter evidence when they deem it fair. Furthermore, parties undergoing arbitration typically agree to conduct some limited form of discovery . In recent times, arbitration has become controversial due to the widespread use of mandatory arbitration clauses. Under these clauses, parties contracting with each other agree to submit any future dispute to arbitration rather than to a court of law. These clauses are often included within contracts of adhesion and are therefore prerequisites to employment. As a result, many people have raised questions regarding whether the constitutional right of employees to a trial are being infringed upon. Nonetheless, the Supreme Court has upheld the validity of mandatory arbitration clauses on multiple occasions . Notably, the Federal Arbitration Act does not apply to seamen, railroad employees, or any other class of workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce. This exception applies regardless of whether the worker is considered an employee or an independent contractor (see: New Prime Inc. v. Oliveira , 586 U.S. (2019) . [Last reviewed in March of 2025 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex securities THE LEGAL PROCESS alternative dispute resolution arbitration courts wex definitions civil procedure courts and procedure legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates court proceedings in Singapore, removing the option of arbitration, which contradicts the principles of party autonomy. The Law Explanation states that this undermines the parties' right to agree on a method of dispute resolution. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that "arbitration refers to an alternative dispute resolution method where the parties in dispute agree to have their case heard by a qualified arbitrator out of court." The changed text removes this option, creating a legal contradiction. This falls under the definition of Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction as it sets a requirement (court proceedings only) that violates the principle of freedom to choose arbitration.
